mappings.rationaleAI: Art theory examining the nature, function, and interpretation of the arts addresses the philosophy of art (A), diverse conceptions of the scope of art (A.1), theories concerning the nature and functions of art including mimetic and formalist theories (A.2), and the creative process (A.3).

Aesthetics examining the nature of art, beauty, taste, and aesthetic experience addresses the philosophy of art (A), diverse conceptions of the scope of art (A.1), and theories concerning the nature and effects of art (A.2).

Philosophical investigation of beauty as a real and universal value addresses the philosophy of art (A). Arguments about the rationality of aesthetic judgment address theories concerning the nature and effects of art (A.2).
Psychological perspectives on creativity from prehistoric cave paintings to modern innovation directly address the making of works of art and the creative process (A.3).
Imagination as a cognitive power for creative activity in both artistic and scientific domains addresses the making of works of art and the creative process (A.3).
Art history studying the development of visual arts and the emergence of global art histories addresses style in the arts (B.4), particularly the varieties of style including period styles (B.4.b).

Design encompassing industrial and cultural design addresses major distinctions among kinds of art (B.1), including the distinction between useful and fine art (B.1.a) and art involving the manipulation of physical matter (B.1.b).

Literary theory analyzing literature and cultural production addresses theories concerning the nature and functions of art (A.2). Structuralism and speech act theory connect to art using notational devices (B.1.d).
Contemporary art covering the global art scene, market dynamics, and museum branding addresses style in the arts (B.4) and varieties of style including period styles (B.4.b).
Modern art covering avant-garde movements and critical debates about modernity addresses style in the arts (B.4) and varieties of style including personal, school, and period styles (B.4.b).

The avant-garde as a concept examining the relationship between ideology and art addresses theories concerning the nature and functions of art (A.2). Its development within modernity addresses style in the arts (B.4).
Global folk music traditions spanning diverse cultures address the characterization of works of art by cultural circumstances of production: the folk arts (B.3).
Ethnomusicology studying music across geographic and cultural diversity addresses the characterization of works of art by cultural or social circumstances: primitive, folk, and popular arts (B.3).
Theatre integrating dance, literature, music, painting, sculpture, and architecture addresses major distinctions among kinds of art (B.1), particularly performers as interpreters or creators (B.1.c).
Music covering performance practice, composition, and notation addresses performers as interpreters or creators (B.1.c) and art using notational devices (B.1.d).
Pan-European Romantic movement spanning literature, music, and painting addresses style in the arts (B.4) and period styles (B.4.b).
Modernism transforming art, literature, and architecture addresses theories concerning the nature and functions of art (A.2) and style in the arts including period styles (B.4, B.4.b).

Postmodernism's skepticism toward grand narratives addresses theories concerning the nature of art (A.2). Its cultural manifestations across philosophy, arts, and architecture address style including period styles (B.4, B.4.b).
Dada and Surrealism as avant-garde movements addressing art through psychoanalysis and the readymade concept address theories concerning the nature of art (A.2) and style in the arts (B.4).
Anthropology studying cultural systems through fieldwork provides context for understanding primitive, folk, and popular arts by reference to cultural circumstances (B.3).
Critical theory covering the culture industry and reification addresses theories concerning the nature, functions, and effects of art (A.2), particularly pragmatic theories of art's social function.
German philosophy covering aesthetics and the relationship between artistic and scientific understanding addresses the philosophy of art (A) and diverse theories concerning art (A.2).
Renaissance art covering painting, sculpture, and decorative arts in cultural context addresses style in the arts (B.4) and period styles (B.4.b).

Covers the relationship between form and function and how buildings acquire cultural meaning — relevant to useful and fine art distinctions, manipulation of physical matter, and other distinctions among art kinds.
Covers comedy as spanning written forms and performance traditions from drama to stand-up — relevant to performers as interpreters of works and characterization of art by cultural circumstances.
Covers theoretical approaches from structuralism to semiotics applied to myth as cultural production — relevant to characterization of works of art by cultural and social circumstances.
Covers structuralism, semiology, and post-structuralist textual theory analyzing cultural phenomena — relevant to theories concerning the nature and functions of art and style in the arts.
Covers existentialism's relationship to the arts including literature and theater — relevant to expressive theories of art.
